---
title: "Factors"
---

This extra chapter outlines what factors are and why we might use them.

We'll start by using an example we've covered elsewhere in this book, the months of a year. Then we'll build our own factor using age ranges in the [Texas Immunization Registry (ImmTrac2)](https://www.dshs.texas.gov/immunizations/data/program-data) data, which tracks how often kids get immunized each year in a given county..

## What is a factor

A factor is a categorical variable (words) where the values have a set order. The classic example we'll use here is months of the year: January, February, March, etc. Months are words for sure, but they have an order. We want to see them listed starting with January and ending in December, instead of alphabetically starting with April, then August, etc. Days of the week are similar.

Survey results are another common use for factors, especially when considering ranges of categories. The example we'll get into here is age groups, where we want these categories like "2-8 years" and "18+ years" to be listed in age order instead of alphabetically, which would put the `1` in "18" first. But you could see the same with salary ranges and other categories you want listed in a specific order that isn't alphabetical.

<img src="https://forcats.tidyverse.org/logo.png" height="120px" style="margin-left: 10px; float:right;"/>

There is a tidyverse package called [forcats](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/) that provides functions to work with factors. The use cases for these can get esoteric, so we'll concentrate on a couple that are common in our work as journalists.

> The package name "forcats" is an anagram for "factors". Apparently Hadley Wickham is a cat guy with a dad's taste in jokes.

## Getting started

We have to import our data and do some cleanup, like with any project. We'll just do that here without much explanation to avoid going too far astray, but it is also an example of using the [readxl package](https://readxl.tidyverse.org/).

### Setup

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(readxl)
library(scales)
```

### Import & fix number of vaccinations

Import the data from the spreadsheet and clean the names. Also, the number of vaccinations is redacted with `**` if there are fewer than five, so we have to fix that column.

```{r}
#| label: import
#| warning: false
#| message: false

tir_raw <- read_excel(
  "data-original/immtrac2-doses-administered-for-web.xlsx",
  sheet = "Data",
  skip = 1) |> 
  clean_names()

tir <- tir_raw |> 
  mutate(number_of_vaccination = parse_number(number_of_vaccination))
```

## Months as a factor

### Let's show the problem

In our data we have a column for `year` and `month`, but they are just text. If we try to summarize the total number of vaccinations by month, the order doesn't make sense because the months are alphabetical.

```{r}
tir |> 
  group_by(month, year) |> 
  summarize(total_vacc = sum(number_of_vaccination, na.rm = T),
            .groups = "drop") |> 
  arrange(year, month) |> 
  filter(year == "2020")
```

Our brain wants to see this starting with January, not April. If we tried to chart this it would also put the months in alphabetical order.

### Create a "real" date and month

In other chapters of this book, including in [Grouping by dates](group-dates.qmd), we used functions from the [lubridate package](lubridate.tidyverse.org) to create a smart "month" variable that ordered them in the correct way, starting with January and ending in December.

We'll do that again here, but we don't really have a full date to pluck from here, so we'll have to manufacture it from the month and year, also using lubridate functions.

```{r}
tir_mo <- tir |> 
  mutate(
    mo_yr = my(paste(month, year)), # <1>
    mo = month(mo_yr, label = T, abbr = F), # <2>
    .after = month # <3>
  )

# just showing the top of the tibble
tir_mo |> head()
```

1. In this line, I'm creating a "real" date by using the `paste()` function to put together the "month" and "year" and feeding it to lubridate's `my()` function. I need this "real" date so I can use lubridate's `month()` function to get the "ordered" month.
2. Here we use `month()` to pluck our our ordered month into a new variable.
3. This `.after =` argument just specifies where in the tibble add these new columns. I put them near the original one so we could see them.

Note the data type "<ord>" for `mo` compared to "<chr>" we had for `month`. That data type indicates this is a special "ordered factor". When we create our own, this data type will be "<fct>".

I'll just do a little cleanup to drop and rename some things. I'm keeping out `mo_yr` as sometimes a full date is flexible with charting. You can probably figure out the specifics through the function names.

```{r}
tir_dates <- tir_mo |> 
  select(-month) |> 
  rename(month = mo) |> 
  relocate(mo_yr, .before = year)

tir_dates |> glimpse()
```
### Show the result

Now let's go back to that original summary we were doing.

```{r}
tir_dates |> 
  group_by(month, year) |> 
  summarize(total_vacc = sum(number_of_vaccination, na.rm = T),
            .groups = "drop") |> 
  arrange(year, month) |> 
  filter(year == "2020")
```

Now these are ordered the way we want them.

## Creating a new factor

Now we have a similar problem with with our age categories, but our solution will be a little different. We have a variable called `age_at_vaccination` that are really categories of ages.

### Ages out of order

Let's look at this if we summarize the number of vaccinations based on this age category.

```{r}
tir_dates |> 
  group_by(age_at_vaccination) |> 
  summarize(total_vacc = sum(number_of_vaccination, na.rm = T))
```

The result is ordered by `age_at_vacciation` alphabetically, but that isn't a logical way to order this. Starting at "0 to 24 months" is fine, but we want that to be followed by "2-8 years", not "18+ years". The solution is to create a version of this as a factor.

### Using fct()

There are a number of ways that you can create a factor from an existing variable, including a series of Base R functions built around [`factor()`](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/base/versions/3.6.2/topics/factor) that include `as.factor()`.

However, like many other times in this book, we will favor using tidyverse functions designed to work together, like `fct()`, `as_factor()` and other functions from the [forcats package](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/reference/index.html).

Since we are changing or creating data, we'll need to use our functions with a `mutate()` function. The `fct()` function turns a variable into a factor based on the unique values in the data. In this case, it does exactly what we want.

```{r}
tir_fct <- tir_dates |> 
  mutate(
    age_fct = fct(age_at_vaccination), # <1>
    .after = age_at_vaccination
  )

tir_fct |> head()
```

1. This is where we create our new variable. By default the `fct()` function creates the order (or "levels") of the factor in the order it finds them in the data. (In the next example we'll set our own order.)

You can see the data type for our new variable`age_fct` is called `<fct>` instead of `<chr>` in the original.

### Resulting factor

So let's look at that summary again, using the new `age_fct` variable.

```{r}
tir_fct_sums <- tir_fct |> 
  group_by(age_fct) |> 
  summarize(total_vacc = sum(number_of_vaccination, na.rm = T))

tir_fct_sums
```

## Set factor order manually

Sometimes our data is not provided to us in the proper order, so we set the order ourselves using the `level =` argument. It's pretty strict that you list all the options carefully or it will error.

In this case we will create the levels in reverse order of our age groups, just to show this concept.

```{r}
tir_rev <- tir_dates |> 
  mutate(
    age_fct_man = fct(age_at_vaccination,
                      levels = c("18+ years",
                                 "9-17 years",
                                 "2-8 years",
                                 "0 to 24 months"))
  )
```


Our summary is ordered like this now, from oldest to youngest:

```{r}
tir_rev_sums <- tir_rev |> 
  group_by(age_fct_man) |> 
  summarize(total_vacc = sum(number_of_vaccination, na.rm = T))

tir_rev_sums
```

There are lots of other ways to use factors and change their levels, but ordering categories like this has been the most prevalent use for me within journalism.

If you need to learn more, check out the [forcats package](https://forcats.tidyverse.org/articles/forcats.html) and the [Factors chapter](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/factors.html) in the R for Data Science book.
